<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Manji-no-sekibutsu]][[ja:万治の石仏]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Manji-no-sekibutsu]][[ja:万治の石仏]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Manji-no-sekibutsu]][[ja:万治の石仏]]
{{Infobox artwork | image_file = Yashiro, Shimosuwa, Suwa District, Nagano Prefecture 393-0000, Japan - panoramio.jpg | painting_alignment = right | image_size = 300px | title = Manji no Sekibutsu | caption = Front view | year = {{ill|1660|en|1660|WD=Q7001|lt=1660}} (Manji 3) | type = {{ill|Stone Buddha|ja|石仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Stone Buddha|qq=}} | material = {{ill|Andesite|en|Andesite|WD=Q181908|lt=Andesite}} (hornblende-pyroxene andesite) | height_metric = 2.6 | width_metric = 3.8 | metric_unit = m | accession = | city = {{ill|Shimosuwa|en|Shimosuwa|WD=Q1204211|lt=Shimosuwa, Nagano}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|en|Nagano Prefecture|WD=Q127877|lt=Nagano Prefecture}} | museum = | website = {{URL|1=https://www.town.shimosuwa.lg.jp/www/contents/1001000000750/index.html|2=Manji no Sekibutsu (Shimosuwa Town)}} }}
'''Manji no Sekibutsu''' (万治の石仏) is a {{ill|Stone Buddha|lt=Stone Buddha|lt=Stone Buddha statue|ja|石仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/stone Buddha statue|qq=}} located in {{ill|Shimosuwa|en|Shimosuwa|WD=Q1204211|lt=Shimosuwa}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|en|Nagano Prefecture|WD=Q127877|lt=Nagano Prefecture}}, Japan. It was created in the early Edo period in {{ill|1660|en|1660|WD=Q7001|lt=1660}} (Manji 3). Owned by Shimosuwa Town, it has been designated a Municipal Cultural Property since {{ill|1982|en|1982|WD=Q2436|lt=1982}} (Shōwa 57) under the name "Manji no Sekibutsu (Mitarashi no Sekibutsu)."<ref name=town>[http://www.town.shimosuwa.lg.jp/www/contents/1001000000750/index.html Manji no Sekibutsu] - Shimosuwa Town, accessed February 11, 2021.</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.town.shimosuwa.lg.jp/www/contents/1496969553133/files/5-106.pdf |title=Municipal Cultural Property No. 34 |access-date=2023/8/20 |publisher=Shimosuwa Town}}</ref> It is situated across the {{ill|Togawa River|ja|砥川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Togawa River|qq=}} from the {{ill|Suwa Taisha Shimosha Spring Shrine|ja|諏訪大社#下社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Taisha Shimosha Spring Shrine|1=Suwa taisha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q218813}}. The local area name "Sekibutsu" is derived from this statue.
The stone Buddha gained national recognition in the {{ill|1970s|en|1970s|WD=Q35014|lt=1970s}} when it was introduced by the artist {{ill|Tarō Okamoto|en|Tarō Okamoto|WD=Q983942|lt=Taro Okamoto}}.<ref name=kanko>[https://shimosuwaonsen.jp/万治の石仏/ Manji no Sekibutsu] - Shimosuwa Tourism Association, accessed February 11, 2021.</ref>
"Manji no Sekibutsu" is also a registered trademark owned by the Shimosuwa Chamber of Commerce.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|url=https://www.j-platpat.inpit.go.jp/c1800/TR/JP-1975-111530/F9955187CD2A8CF63F94F7E0BFD8C0A6B6A2FE9ED1871582677DE84CB61BA02A/40/ja |title=Trademark Registration No. 1326194 |access-date=2023/7/8 |publisher=National Center for Industrial Property Information and Training}}</ref>
== Overview == [[File:Manji-no sekibutsu (stone buddha) , 万治の石仏 - panoramio (20).jpg|thumb|Inscription "Namu Amida Butsu Manji 3rd Year, 11th Month, 1st Day, Ganshu Myōyo Jokō, Shinyo Hiroharu"]]
The stone Buddha measures 2.6 meters in height, 3.8 meters in width, and 3.7 meters in depth. It is made of andesite (hornblende-pyroxene andesite), with a Buddha head approximately 65 centimeters tall placed on a massive stone body.<ref name=kanko/><ref name=":4" /> The front of the body features a carved {{ill|Amitābha|en|Amitābha|WD=Q236242|lt=Amida Nyorai}} seated in {{ill|mudra|lt=Dhyanamudra|ja|印相#阿弥陀如来の印相|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mudra|1=mudra|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q208030}}.<ref name=town/><ref name=":4" />
The robe is adorned with symbols such as {{ill|Swastika|en|Swastika|WD=Q45513|lt=Swastika}} (right-facing), the sun, thunder, clouds, rocks, and the moon, reflecting esoteric {{ill|Mikkyō|en|Mikkyō|WD=Q336102|lt=Mikkyō}} mandalas. These elements are attributed to the "One Body, Two Buddhas" doctrine of a sect of sculpting priests from the {{ill|Jōdo-shū|en|Jōdo-shū|WD=Q912208|lt=Jōdo-shū}}, combining {{ill|Amitābha|en|Amitābha|WD=Q236242|lt=Amida Nyorai}} with {{ill|Vairocana|en|Vairocana|WD=Q239847|lt=Dainichi Nyorai}}.<ref name=town/><ref name=":4" />
== Legend == A local legend recounts that during the construction of a large {{ill|Torii|en|Torii|WD=Q234731|lt=torii}} for Suwa Taisha in {{ill|1657|en|1657|WD=Q6987|lt=1657}} (Meireki 3), a stonecutter attempted to use the current stone Buddha as material. However, upon striking it with a chisel, the stone bled. Afraid of divine retribution, the construction ceased. The stone was later consecrated as an Amida Nyorai.<ref name=town/><ref name=kanko/>
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Manji-no-sekibutsu01n4592.jpg|Front view File:Manji-no-sekibutsu06n4592.jpg|Rear view File:Manji-no-sekibutsu05n4592.jpg|Face File:Manji-no-sekibutsu07s4592.jpg|Hand mudra </gallery>
== Access == * 16 minutes by bus from {{ill|Shimo-Suwa Station|en|Shimo-Suwa Station|WD=Q7496937|lt=Shimosuwa Station}} (Miyanoue bus stop) * {{ill|Japan National Route 20|en|Japan National Route 20|WD=Q430608|lt=National Route 20 (Japan)}}
== Nearby == * {{ill|Suwa Taisha Shimosha Spring Shrine|ja|諏訪大社#下社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Taisha Shimosha Spring Shrine|1=Suwa taisha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q218813}} * {{ill|Jiunji Temple (Nagano)|ja|慈雲寺 (長野県下諏訪町)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jiunji Temple (Nagano)|1=Jiun-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11495049}} * {{ill|Suigetsu Park|ja|水月公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suigetsu Park|1=Suigetsu Park|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109073780}} * Grave of {{ill|Yamatokentomotsu|ja|大和監物|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatokentomotsu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Yajoseki|ja|矢除石|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yajoseki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
== References == {{Reflist|25em}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Landmarks of Shimosuwa|ja|下諏訪町#名所・旧跡・観光スポット・祭事・催事|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Landmarks of Shimosuwa|1=Shimosuwa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1204211}} * {{ill|Tarō Okamoto|en|Tarō Okamoto|WD=Q983942|lt=Taro Okamoto}} * {{ill|Jirō Nitta|en|Jirō Nitta|WD=Q3179274|lt=Jiro Nitta}} * {{ill|Kazuo Ichikawa|ja|市川一雄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazuo Ichikawa|1=Ichikawa Kazuo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q106478628}}
== External Links == * [https://shimosuwaonsen.jp/万治の石仏/ Manji no Sekibutsu] - Shimosuwa Tourism Association * [https://www.j-platpat.inpit.go.jp/c1800/TR/JP-1975-111530/F9955187CD2A8CF63F94F7E0BFD8C0A6B6A2FE9ED1871582677DE84CB61BA02A/40/ja Trademark Registration No. 1326194] - Industrial Property Digital Platform

These elements are attributed to the "One Body, Two Buddhas" doctrine of a sect of sculpting priests from the {{ill|Jōdo-shū|en|Jōdo-shū|WD=Q912208|lt=Jōdo-shū}}, combining {{ill|Amitābha|en|Amitābha|WD=Q236242|lt=Amida Nyorai}} with {{ill|Vairocana|en|Vairocana|WD=Q239847|lt=Dainichi Nyorai}}.<ref name=town/><ref name=":4" />
== Legend == A local legend recounts that during the construction of a large {{ill|Torii|en|Torii|WD=Q234731|lt=torii}} for Suwa Taisha in {{ill|1657|en|1657|WD=Q6987|lt=1657}} (Meireki 3), a stonecutter attempted to use the current stone Buddha as material. However, upon striking it with a chisel, the stone bled. Afraid of divine retribution, the construction ceased. The stone was later consecrated as an Amida Nyorai.<ref name=town/><ref name=kanko/>
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Manji-no-sekibutsu01n4592.jpg|Front view File:Manji-no-sekibutsu06n4592.jpg|Rear view File:Manji-no-sekibutsu05n4592.jpg|Face File:Manji-no-sekibutsu07s4592.jpg|Hand mudra </gallery>
== Access == * 16 minutes by bus from {{ill|Shimo-Suwa Station|en|Shimo-Suwa Station|WD=Q7496937|lt=Shimosuwa Station}} (Miyanoue bus stop) * {{ill|Japan National Route 20|en|Japan National Route 20|WD=Q430608|lt=National Route 20 (Japan)}}
== Nearby == * {{ill|Suwa Taisha Shimosha Spring Shrine|ja|諏訪大社#下社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Taisha Shimosha Spring Shrine|1=Suwa taisha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q218813}} * {{ill|Jiunji Temple (Nagano)|ja|慈雲寺 (長野県下諏訪町)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jiunji Temple (Nagano)|1=Jiun-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11495049}} * {{ill|Suigetsu Park|ja|水月公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suigetsu Park|1=Suigetsu Park|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109073780}} * Grave of {{ill|Yamatokentomotsu|ja|大和監物|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatokentomotsu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Yajoseki|ja|矢除石|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yajoseki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Manji-no-sekibutsu]][[ja:万治の石仏]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Manji-no-sekibutsu]][[ja:万治の石仏]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Manji-no-sekibutsu]][[ja:万治の石仏]]
{{Infobox artwork | image_file = Yashiro, Shimosuwa, Suwa District, Nagano Prefecture 393-0000, Japan - panoramio.jpg | painting_alignment = right | image_size = 300px | title = Manji no Sekibutsu | caption = Front view | year = {{ill|1660|en|1660|WD=Q7001|lt=1660|1=1660}} (Manji 3) | type = {{ill|Stone Buddha|ja|石仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Stone Buddha|qq=}} | material = {{ill|Andesite|en|Andesite|WD=Q181908|lt=Andesite|1=Andesite}} (hornblende-pyroxene andesite) | height_metric = 2.6 | width_metric = 3.8 | metric_unit = m | accession = | city = {{ill|Shimosuwa|en|Shimosuwa|WD=Q1204211|lt=Shimosuwa, Nagano|1=Shimosuwa}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|en|Nagano Prefecture|WD=Q127877|lt=Nagano Prefecture|1=Nagano Prefecture}} | museum = | website = {{URL|1=https://www.town.shimosuwa.lg.jp/www/contents/1001000000750/index.html|2=Manji no Sekibutsu (Shimosuwa Town)}} }}
'''Manji no Sekibutsu''' (万治の石仏) is a {{ill|Stone Buddha|lt=Stone Buddha|lt=Stone Buddha statue|ja|石仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/stone Buddha statue|qq=}} located in {{ill|Shimosuwa|en|Shimosuwa|WD=Q1204211|lt=Shimosuwa|1=Shimosuwa}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|en|Nagano Prefecture|WD=Q127877|lt=Nagano Prefecture|1=Nagano Prefecture}}, Japan. It was created in the early Edo period in {{ill|1660|en|1660|WD=Q7001|lt=1660|1=1660}} (Manji 3). Owned by Shimosuwa Town, it has been designated a Municipal Cultural Property since {{ill|1982|en|1982|WD=Q2436|lt=1982|1=1982}} (Shōwa 57) under the name "Manji no Sekibutsu (Mitarashi no Sekibutsu)."<ref name=town>[http://www.town.shimosuwa.lg.jp/www/contents/1001000000750/index.html Manji no Sekibutsu] - Shimosuwa Town, accessed February 11, 2021.</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.town.shimosuwa.lg.jp/www/contents/1496969553133/files/5-106.pdf |title=Municipal Cultural Property No. 34 |access-date=2023/8/20 |publisher=Shimosuwa Town}}</ref> It is situated across the {{ill|Togawa River|ja|砥川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Togawa River|qq=}} from the {{ill|Suwa Taisha Shimosha Spring Shrine|ja|諏訪大社#下社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Taisha Shimosha Spring Shrine|1=Suwa-taisha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q218813}}. The local area name "Sekibutsu" is derived from this statue.
The stone Buddha gained national recognition in the {{ill|1970s|en|1970s|WD=Q35014|lt=1970s|1=1970s}} when it was introduced by the artist {{ill|Tarō Okamoto|en|Tarō Okamoto|WD=Q983942|lt=Taro Okamoto|1=Tarō Okamoto}}.<ref name=kanko>[https://shimosuwaonsen.jp/万治の石仏/ Manji no Sekibutsu] - Shimosuwa Tourism Association, accessed February 11, 2021.</ref>
"Manji no Sekibutsu" is also a registered trademark owned by the Shimosuwa Chamber of Commerce.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|url=https://www.j-platpat.inpit.go.jp/c1800/TR/JP-1975-111530/F9955187CD2A8CF63F94F7E0BFD8C0A6B6A2FE9ED1871582677DE84CB61BA02A/40/ja |title=Trademark Registration No. 1326194 |access-date=2023/7/8 |publisher=National Center for Industrial Property Information and Training}}</ref>
== Overview == [[File:Manji-no sekibutsu (stone buddha) , 万治の石仏 - panoramio (20).jpg|thumb|Inscription "Namu Amida Butsu Manji 3rd Year, 11th Month, 1st Day, Ganshu Myōyo Jokō, Shinyo Hiroharu"]]
The stone Buddha measures 2.6 meters in height, 3.8 meters in width, and 3.7 meters in depth. It is made of andesite (hornblende-pyroxene andesite), with a Buddha head approximately 65 centimeters tall placed on a massive stone body.<ref name=kanko/><ref name=":4" /> The front of the body features a carved {{ill|Amitābha|en|Amitābha|WD=Q236242|lt=Amida Nyorai|1=Amitābha}} seated in {{ill|mudra|lt=Dhyanamudra|ja|印相#阿弥陀如来の印相|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mudra|1=Mudra|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q208030}}.<ref name=town/><ref name=":4" />
The robe is adorned with symbols such as {{ill|Swastika|en|Swastika|WD=Q45513|lt=Swastika|1=Swastika}} (right-facing), the sun, thunder, clouds, rocks, and the moon, reflecting esoteric {{ill|Mikkyō|en|Mikkyō|WD=Q336102|lt=Mikkyō|1=Mikkyō}} mandalas. These elements are attributed to the "One Body, Two Buddhas" doctrine of a sect of sculpting priests from the {{ill|Jōdo-shū|en|Jōdo-shū|WD=Q912208|lt=Jōdo-shū|1=Jōdo-shū}}, combining {{ill|Amitābha|en|Amitābha|WD=Q236242|lt=Amida Nyorai|1=Amitābha}} with {{ill|Vairocana|en|Vairocana|WD=Q239847|lt=Dainichi Nyorai|1=Vairocana}}.<ref name=town/><ref name=":4" />
== Legend == A local legend recounts that during the construction of a large {{ill|Torii|en|Torii|WD=Q234731|lt=torii|1=Torii}} for Suwa Taisha in {{ill|1657|en|1657|WD=Q6987|lt=1657|1=1657}} (Meireki 3), a stonecutter attempted to use the current stone Buddha as material. However, upon striking it with a chisel, the stone bled. Afraid of divine retribution, the construction ceased. The stone was later consecrated as an Amida Nyorai.<ref name=town/><ref name=kanko/>
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Manji-no-sekibutsu01n4592.jpg|Front view File:Manji-no-sekibutsu06n4592.jpg|Rear view File:Manji-no-sekibutsu05n4592.jpg|Face File:Manji-no-sekibutsu07s4592.jpg|Hand mudra </gallery>
== Access == * 16 minutes by bus from {{ill|Shimo-Suwa Station|en|Shimo-Suwa Station|WD=Q7496937|lt=Shimosuwa Station|1=Shimo-Suwa Station}} (Miyanoue bus stop) * {{ill|Japan National Route 20|en|Japan National Route 20|WD=Q430608|lt=National Route 20 (Japan)|1=Japan National Route 20}}
== Nearby == * {{ill|Suwa Taisha Shimosha Spring Shrine|ja|諏訪大社#下社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Taisha Shimosha Spring Shrine|1=Suwa-taisha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q218813}} * {{ill|Jiunji Temple (Nagano)|ja|慈雲寺 (長野県下諏訪町)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jiunji Temple (Nagano)|1=Jiunji (Nagano Prefecture Shimosuwa Town)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11495049}} * {{ill|Suigetsu Park|ja|水月公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suigetsu Park|1=Q109073780|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109073780}} * Grave of {{ill|Yamatokentomotsu|ja|大和監物|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatokentomotsu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Yajoseki|ja|矢除石|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yajoseki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}

<!--Manji Stone Buddha--><!--Manji Stone Buddha--><!--Manji Stone Buddha--><!--Manji Stone Buddha--> <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Manji-no-sekibutsu}} {{wikidata link|Q11353878}} [[Category:1660 establishments]] [[Category:1660 in Japan]] [[Category:Art of the Edo period]] [[Category:Buddha statues in Japan]] [[Category:Buddhism in the Edo period]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Japanese legends]] [[Category:Municipal History of Shimosuwa]] [[Category:Municipal Tangible Cultural Properties in Nagano Prefecture]] [[Category:Okamoto Tarō]] [[Category:Statues of Amitābha Buddha]] [[Category:Stone Buddha statues]] [[Category:Suwa faith]] [[Category:Tourist attractions in Nagano Prefecture]] [[Category:Trademarks]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:pages]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Manji-no-sekibutsu]][[ja:万治の石仏]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Manji-no-sekibutsu]][[ja:万治の石仏]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[Category:sn_crud|commons:Category:Manji-no-sekibutsu]][[ja:万治の石仏]]
{{Infobox artwork | image_file = Yashiro, Shimosuwa, Suwa District, Nagano Prefecture 393-0000, Japan - panoramio.jpg | painting_alignment = right | image_size = 300px | title = Manji no Sekibutsu | caption = Front view | year = {{ill|1660|en|1660|WD=Q7001|lt=1660|1=1660}} (Manji 3) | type = {{ill|Stone Buddha|ja|石仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Stone Buddha|qq=}} | material = {{ill|Andesite|en|Andesite|WD=Q181908|lt=Andesite|1=Andesite}} (hornblende-pyroxene andesite) | height_metric = 2.6 | width_metric = 3.8 | metric_unit = m | accession = | city = {{ill|Shimosuwa|en|Shimosuwa|WD=Q1204211|lt=Shimosuwa, Nagano|1=Shimosuwa}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|en|Nagano Prefecture|WD=Q127877|lt=Nagano Prefecture|1=Nagano Prefecture}} | museum = | website = {{URL|1=https://www.town.shimosuwa.lg.jp/www/contents/1001000000750/index.html|2=Manji no Sekibutsu (Shimosuwa Town)}} }}
'''Manji no Sekibutsu''' (万治の石仏) is a {{ill|Stone Buddha|lt=Stone Buddha|lt=Stone Buddha statue|ja|石仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/stone Buddha statue|qq=}} located in {{ill|Shimosuwa|en|Shimosuwa|WD=Q1204211|lt=Shimosuwa|1=Shimosuwa}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|en|Nagano Prefecture|WD=Q127877|lt=Nagano Prefecture|1=Nagano Prefecture}}, Japan. It was created in the early Edo period in {{ill|1660|en|1660|WD=Q7001|lt=1660|1=1660}} (Manji 3). Owned by Shimosuwa Town, it has been designated a Municipal Cultural Property since {{ill|1982|en|1982|WD=Q2436|lt=1982|1=1982}} (Shōwa 57) under the name "Manji no Sekibutsu (Mitarashi no Sekibutsu)."<ref name=town>[http://www.town.shimosuwa.lg.jp/www/contents/1001000000750/index.html Manji no Sekibutsu] - Shimosuwa Town, accessed February 11, 2021.</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.town.shimosuwa.lg.jp/www/contents/1496969553133/files/5-106.pdf |title=Municipal Cultural Property No. 34 |access-date=2023/8/20 |publisher=Shimosuwa Town}}</ref> It is situated across the {{ill|Togawa River|ja|砥川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Togawa River|qq=}} from the {{ill|Suwa Taisha Shimosha Spring Shrine|ja|諏訪大社#下社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Taisha Shimosha Spring Shrine|1=Suwa-taisha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q218813}}. The local area name "Sekibutsu" is derived from this statue.
The stone Buddha gained national recognition in the {{ill|1970s|en|1970s|WD=Q35014|lt=1970s|1=1970s}} when it was introduced by the artist {{ill|Tarō Okamoto|en|Tarō Okamoto|WD=Q983942|lt=Taro Okamoto|1=Tarō Okamoto}}.<ref name=kanko>[https://shimosuwaonsen.jp/万治の石仏/ Manji no Sekibutsu] - Shimosuwa Tourism Association, accessed February 11, 2021.</ref>
"Manji no Sekibutsu" is also a registered trademark owned by the Shimosuwa Chamber of Commerce.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|url=https://www.j-platpat.inpit.go.jp/c1800/TR/JP-1975-111530/F9955187CD2A8CF63F94F7E0BFD8C0A6B6A2FE9ED1871582677DE84CB61BA02A/40/ja |title=Trademark Registration No. 1326194 |access-date=2023/7/8 |publisher=National Center for Industrial Property Information and Training}}</ref>
== Overview == [[File:Manji-no sekibutsu (stone buddha) , 万治の石仏 - panoramio (20).jpg|thumb|Inscription "Namu Amida Butsu Manji 3rd Year, 11th Month, 1st Day, Ganshu Myōyo Jokō, Shinyo Hiroharu"]]
The stone Buddha measures 2.6 meters in height, 3.8 meters in width, and 3.7 meters in depth. It is made of andesite (hornblende-pyroxene andesite), with a Buddha head approximately 65 centimeters tall placed on a massive stone body.<ref name=kanko/><ref name=":4" /> The front of the body features a carved {{ill|Amitābha|en|Amitābha|WD=Q236242|lt=Amida Nyorai|1=Amitābha}} seated in {{ill|mudra|lt=Dhyanamudra|ja|印相#阿弥陀如来の印相|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mudra|1=Mudra|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q208030}}.<ref name=town/><ref name=":4" />
The robe is adorned with symbols such as {{ill|Swastika|en|Swastika|WD=Q45513|lt=Swastika|1=Swastika}} (right-facing), the sun, thunder, clouds, rocks, and the moon, reflecting esoteric {{ill|Mikkyō|en|Mikkyō|WD=Q336102|lt=Mikkyō|1=Mikkyō}} mandalas. These elements are attributed to the "One Body, Two Buddhas" doctrine of a sect of sculpting priests from the {{ill|Jōdo-shū|en|Jōdo-shū|WD=Q912208|lt=Jōdo-shū|1=Jōdo-shū}}, combining {{ill|Amitābha|en|Amitābha|WD=Q236242|lt=Amida Nyorai|1=Amitābha}} with {{ill|Vairocana|en|Vairocana|WD=Q239847|lt=Dainichi Nyorai|1=Vairocana}}.<ref name=town/><ref name=":4" />
== Legend == A local legend recounts that during the construction of a large {{ill|Torii|en|Torii|WD=Q234731|lt=torii|1=Torii}} for Suwa Taisha in {{ill|1657|en|1657|WD=Q6987|lt=1657|1=1657}} (Meireki 3), a stonecutter attempted to use the current stone Buddha as material. However, upon striking it with a chisel, the stone bled. Afraid of divine retribution, the construction ceased. The stone was later consecrated as an Amida Nyorai.<ref name=town/><ref name=kanko/>
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Manji-no-sekibutsu01n4592.jpg|Front view File:Manji-no-sekibutsu06n4592.jpg|Rear view File:Manji-no-sekibutsu05n4592.jpg|Face File:Manji-no-sekibutsu07s4592.jpg|Hand mudra </gallery>
== Access == * 16 minutes by bus from {{ill|Shimo-Suwa Station|en|Shimo-Suwa Station|WD=Q7496937|lt=Shimosuwa Station|1=Shimo-Suwa Station}} (Miyanoue bus stop) * {{ill|Japan National Route 20|en|Japan National Route 20|WD=Q430608|lt=National Route 20 (Japan)|1=Japan National Route 20}}
== Nearby == * {{ill|Suwa Taisha Shimosha Spring Shrine|ja|諏訪大社#下社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Taisha Shimosha Spring Shrine|1=Suwa-taisha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q218813}} * {{ill|Jiunji Temple (Nagano)|ja|慈雲寺 (長野県下諏訪町)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jiunji Temple (Nagano)|1=Jiunji (Nagano Prefecture Shimosuwa Town)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11495049}} * {{ill|Suigetsu Park|ja|水月公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suigetsu Park|1=Q109073780|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109073780}} * Grave of {{ill|Yamatokentomotsu|ja|大和監物|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatokentomotsu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Yajoseki|ja|矢除石|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yajoseki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}

The stone Buddha gained national recognition in the {{ill|1970s|en|1970s|WD=Q35014|lt=1970s|1=1970s}} when it was introduced by the artist {{ill|Tarō Okamoto|en|Tarō Okamoto|WD=Q983942|lt=Taro Okamoto|1=Tarō Okamoto}}.<ref name=kanko>[https://shimosuwaonsen.jp/万治の石仏/ Manji no Sekibutsu] - Shimosuwa Tourism Association, accessed February 11, 2021.</ref>
"Manji no Sekibutsu" is also a registered trademark owned by the Shimosuwa Chamber of Commerce.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|url=https://www.j-platpat.inpit.go.jp/c1800/TR/JP-1975-111530/F9955187CD2A8CF63F94F7E0BFD8C0A6B6A2FE9ED1871582677DE84CB61BA02A/40/ja |title=Trademark Registration No. 1326194 |access-date=2023/7/8 |publisher=National Center for Industrial Property Information and Training}}</ref>
== Overview == [[File:Manji-no sekibutsu (stone buddha) , 万治の石仏 - panoramio (20).jpg|thumb|Inscription "Namu Amida Butsu Manji 3rd Year, 11th Month, 1st Day, Ganshu Myōyo Jokō, Shinyo Hiroharu"]]
The stone Buddha measures 2.6 meters in height, 3.8 meters in width, and 3.7 meters in depth. It is made of andesite (hornblende-pyroxene andesite), with a Buddha head approximately 65 centimeters tall placed on a massive stone body.<ref name=kanko/><ref name=":4" /> The front of the body features a carved {{ill|Amitābha|en|Amitābha|WD=Q236242|lt=Amida Nyorai|1=Amitābha}} seated in {{ill|mudra|lt=Dhyanamudra|ja|印相#阿弥陀如来の印相|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mudra|1=Mudra|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q208030}}.<ref name=town/><ref name=":4" />
The robe is adorned with symbols such as {{ill|Swastika|en|Swastika|WD=Q45513|lt=Swastika|1=Swastika}} (right-facing), the sun, thunder, clouds, rocks, and the moon, reflecting esoteric {{ill|Mikkyō|en|Mikkyō|WD=Q336102|lt=Mikkyō|1=Mikkyō}} mandalas. These elements are attributed to the "One Body, Two Buddhas" doctrine of a sect of sculpting priests from the {{ill|Jōdo-shū|en|Jōdo-shū|WD=Q912208|lt=Jōdo-shū|1=Jōdo-shū}}, combining {{ill|Amitābha|en|Amitābha|WD=Q236242|lt=Amida Nyorai|1=Amitābha}} with {{ill|Vairocana|en|Vairocana|WD=Q239847|lt=Dainichi Nyorai|1=Vairocana}}.<ref name=town/><ref name=":4" />
== Legend == A local legend recounts that during the construction of a large {{ill|Torii|en|Torii|WD=Q234731|lt=torii|1=Torii}} for Suwa Taisha in {{ill|1657|en|1657|WD=Q6987|lt=1657|1=1657}} (Meireki 3), a stonecutter attempted to use the current stone Buddha as material. However, upon striking it with a chisel, the stone bled. Afraid of divine retribution, the construction ceased. The stone was later consecrated as an Amida Nyorai.<ref name=town/><ref name=kanko/>
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Manji-no-sekibutsu01n4592.jpg|Front view File:Manji-no-sekibutsu06n4592.jpg|Rear view File:Manji-no-sekibutsu05n4592.jpg|Face File:Manji-no-sekibutsu07s4592.jpg|Hand mudra </gallery>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Manji-no-sekibutsu]][[ja:万治の石仏]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Manji-no-sekibutsu]][[ja:万治の石仏]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:万治の石仏]]
{{Infobox artwork | image_file = Yashiro, Shimosuwa, Suwa District, Nagano Prefecture 393-0000, Japan - panoramio.jpg | painting_alignment = right | image_size = 300px | title = Manji no Sekibutsu | caption = Front view | year = {{ill|1660|en|1660|qid=Q7001|lt=1660|1=1660}} (Manji 3) | type = {{ill|Stone Buddha|ja|石仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Stone Buddha|qq=}} | material = {{ill|Andesite|en|Andesite|qid=Q181908|lt=Andesite|1=Andesite}} (hornblende-pyroxene andesite) | height_metric = 2.6 | width_metric = 3.8 | metric_unit = m | accession = | city = {{ill|Shimosuwa|en|Shimosuwa|qid=Q1204211|lt=Shimosuwa, Nagano|1=Shimosuwa}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|en|Nagano Prefecture|qid=Q127877|lt=Nagano Prefecture|1=Nagano Prefecture}} | museum = | website = {{URL|1=https://www.town.shimosuwa.lg.jp/www/contents/1001000000750/index.html|2=Manji no Sekibutsu (Shimosuwa Town)}} }}
'''Manji no Sekibutsu''' (万治の石仏) is a {{ill|Stone Buddha|lt=Stone Buddha|lt=Stone Buddha statue|ja|石仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/stone Buddha statue|qq=}} located in {{ill|Shimosuwa|en|Shimosuwa|qid=Q1204211|lt=Shimosuwa|1=Shimosuwa}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|en|Nagano Prefecture|qid=Q127877|lt=Nagano Prefecture|1=Nagano Prefecture}}, Japan. It was created in the early Edo period in {{ill|1660|en|1660|qid=Q7001|lt=1660|1=1660}} (Manji 3). Owned by Shimosuwa Town, it has been designated a Municipal Cultural Property since {{ill|1982|en|1982|qid=Q2436|lt=1982|1=1982}} (Shōwa 57) under the name "Manji no Sekibutsu (Mitarashi no Sekibutsu)."<ref name=town>[http://www.town.shimosuwa.lg.jp/www/contents/1001000000750/index.html Manji no Sekibutsu] - Shimosuwa Town, accessed February 11, 2021.</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.town.shimosuwa.lg.jp/www/contents/1496969553133/files/5-106.pdf |title=Municipal Cultural Property No. 34 |access-date=2023/8/20 |publisher=Shimosuwa Town}}</ref> It is situated across the {{ill|Togawa River|ja|砥川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Togawa River|qq=}} from the {{ill|Suwa Taisha Shimosha Spring Shrine|ja|諏訪大社#下社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Taisha Shimosha Spring Shrine|1=Suwa-taisha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q218813}}. The local area name "Sekibutsu" is derived from this statue.
The stone Buddha gained national recognition in the {{ill|1970s|en|1970s|qid=Q35014|lt=1970s|1=1970s}} when it was introduced by the artist {{ill|Tarō Okamoto|en|Tarō Okamoto|qid=Q983942|lt=Taro Okamoto|1=Tarō Okamoto}}.<ref name=kanko>[https://shimosuwaonsen.jp/万治の石仏/ Manji no Sekibutsu] - Shimosuwa Tourism Association, accessed February 11, 2021.</ref>
"Manji no Sekibutsu" is also a registered trademark owned by the Shimosuwa Chamber of Commerce.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|url=https://www.j-platpat.inpit.go.jp/c1800/TR/JP-1975-111530/F9955187CD2A8CF63F94F7E0BFD8C0A6B6A2FE9ED1871582677DE84CB61BA02A/40/ja |title=Trademark Registration No. 1326194 |access-date=2023/7/8 |publisher=National Center for Industrial Property Information and Training}}</ref>
== Overview == [[File:Manji-no sekibutsu (stone buddha) , 万治の石仏 - panoramio (20).jpg|thumb|Inscription "Namu Amida Butsu Manji 3rd Year, 11th Month, 1st Day, Ganshu Myōyo Jokō, Shinyo Hiroharu"]]
The stone Buddha measures 2.6 meters in height, 3.8 meters in width, and 3.7 meters in depth. It is made of andesite (hornblende-pyroxene andesite), with a Buddha head approximately 65 centimeters tall placed on a massive stone body.<ref name=kanko/><ref name=":4" /> The front of the body features a carved {{ill|Amitābha|en|Amitābha|qid=Q236242|lt=Amida Nyorai|1=Amitābha}} seated in {{ill|mudra|lt=Dhyanamudra|ja|印相#阿弥陀如来の印相|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mudra|1=Mudra|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q208030}}.<ref name=town/><ref name=":4" />
The robe is adorned with symbols such as {{ill|Swastika|en|Swastika|qid=Q45513|lt=Swastika|1=Swastika}} (right-facing), the sun, thunder, clouds, rocks, and the moon, reflecting esoteric {{ill|Mikkyō|en|Mikkyō|qid=Q336102|lt=Mikkyō|1=Mikkyō}} mandalas. These elements are attributed to the "One Body, Two Buddhas" doctrine of a sect of sculpting priests from the {{ill|Jōdo-shū|en|Jōdo-shū|qid=Q912208|lt=Jōdo-shū|1=Jōdo-shū}}, combining {{ill|Amitābha|en|Amitābha|qid=Q236242|lt=Amida Nyorai|1=Amitābha}} with {{ill|Vairocana|en|Vairocana|qid=Q239847|lt=Dainichi Nyorai|1=Vairocana}}.<ref name=town/><ref name=":4" />
